title: Final Focus That Extends Impacts Without Comparing Them
topic: Resolved: The United States federal government should substantially reduce its military presence in East Asia.
transcript:
In this final focus, the affirmative is winning on two clear voting issues.

First: fiscal burden. Extend our RAND evidence. The negative never answered our opportunity cost mechanism — that every dollar spent on basing is a dollar not invested in domestic modernization. This argument stands and the impact flows cleanly to us.

Second: escalation risk. Extend our Carnegie Endowment evidence. The deterrence instability warrant was never refuted. The 34 percent provocation spike after joint exercises is uncontested data. This impact — elevated risk of conventional miscalculation — flows affirmative.

Both of our contentions are standing, both impacts are clean, and neither was answered by the negative. Vote affirmative.

expected_argument_components.example_claim: RAND opportunity cost mechanism stands uncontested
expected_argument_components.example_warrant: Every dollar spent on basing cannot be invested in domestic modernization

expected_argument_components.example_claim: Carnegie deterrence instability evidence stands uncontested
expected_argument_components.example_warrant: 34% provocation spike after joint exercises shows deterrence instability

notes: Final focus that extends two contentions cleanly but never compares the two impacts against each other. In a real round, a flow judge needs to know which impact wins if they can only vote for one — the speaker gives them no tool to resolve this. This is the most common final_focus mistake: extend without weighing. Tests no_weighing detection in final_focus context (complement to no_weighing_summary which tests summary context).
